verses.text: Now it came to pass in the third year of Hoshea son of Elah king of Israel, that Hezekiah the son of Ahaz king of Judah began to reign. Twenty and five years old was he when he began to reign; and he reigned twenty and nine years in Jerusalem. His mother's name also was Abi, the daughter of Zachariah. And he did that which was right in the sight of the LORD, according to all that David his father did. Guidance: Hezekiah — a king measured against David himself. Out of the household of the apostate Ahaz comes one of the greatest kings in Judah's history. Hezekiah begins to reign at 25 and reigns 29 years. The verdict is the highest the narrator gives: 'he did that which was right in the sight of the LORD, according to all that David his father did.' Most good Judean kings were rated 'right, but the high places remained'; Hezekiah is rated 'like David' — the gold standard, without the usual qualifier (because, as v4 will show, he actually removed the high places). The chapter is showing us the stunning reversal from the previous chapter: Ahaz, who burned his son and defiled the temple, is succeeded by a son who will be Judah's reformer. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's grace can raise up extraordinary faithfulness from the most unpromising soil — Hezekiah grew up in the court of a child-sacrificing idolater (and may himself have been a brother of the son Ahaz offered to Molech) and yet became 'like David.' The chapter is teaching us that no one is destined to repeat a parent's sin; the LORD can produce a David-like heart in the house of an Ahaz. The chapter is also setting the stage for the central drama of these chapters: a king who trusts the LORD will soon face the most terrifying empire on earth, and his faith — not his father's alliances — will be tested to the breaking point.

He removed the high places, and brake the images, and cut down the groves, and brake in pieces the brasen serpent that Moses had made: for unto those days the children of Israel did burn incense to it: and he called it Nehushtan. Guidance: The reform — and the breaking of Nehushtan. Hezekiah does what no good Judean king before him had done: he 'removed the high places' — the very compromise that qualified every prior good king's record. He also smashed the standing-stones (images), cut down the Asherah-poles (groves), and — most remarkably — 'brake in pieces the brasen serpent that Moses had made.' This was the bronze serpent from Numbers 21:8-9, the one the LORD Himself had instructed Moses to make in the wilderness, by which dying Israelites who looked at it were healed. For centuries it had been preserved as a sacred relic; but by Hezekiah's day the people had begun burning incense to it — turning a God-given memorial into an idol. Hezekiah destroys it and dismissively renames it 'Nehushtan' (roughly, 'a mere piece of bronze' — a brazen thing). The chapter is showing us a reformer with rare spiritual discernment. It is one thing to tear down the obvious idols of Baal; it is far harder to destroy a beloved sacred object that has a genuine, holy history. Hezekiah recognized that even a true relic from Moses' own hand had become an idol, and he had the courage to smash it. The chapter is teaching us a profound and uncomfortable lesson: anything, however good and God-given in its origin, can become an idol when people begin to venerate the object rather than the God it pointed to. The bronze serpent had once been a means of grace; now it was a snare, and the reformer's faithfulness was proved precisely by his willingness to destroy the holy thing that had been turned into an idol. The chapter is teaching us to hold even our most cherished religious objects, traditions, and relics loosely — to love the LORD they point to, not the things themselves, and to be willing to part with anything that has crept between us and Him. (John 3:14 will later redeem the serpent's true meaning: Jesus said the lifted-up serpent prefigured His own lifting up on the cross — the real object of the looking-faith the bronze serpent had only foreshadowed.)

He trusted in the LORD God of Israel; so that after him was none like him among all the kings of Judah, nor any that were before him. For he clave to the LORD, and departed not from following him, but kept his commandments, which the LORD commanded Moses. And the LORD was with him; and he prospered whithersoever he went forth: and he rebelled against the king of Assyria, and served him not. He smote the Philistines, even unto Gaza, and the borders thereof, from the tower of the watchmen to the fenced city. Guidance: The heart of the matter — Hezekiah trusted. The narrator gives the deepest reason for Hezekiah's greatness: 'He trusted in the LORD God of Israel; so that after him was none like him among all the kings of Judah, nor any that were before him.' This is an astonishing superlative — none like him, before or after. (A similar superlative is given to Josiah in 23:25; the two are praised for different supreme qualities — Hezekiah for trust, Josiah for wholehearted return to the law; together they are Judah's two greatest reformers.) The marks of his faith are spelled out: he 'clave to the LORD' (the word for clinging, devoted attachment), 'departed not from following him,' and 'kept his commandments.' The result: 'the LORD was with him; and he prospered whithersoever he went forth.' His trust expressed itself in bold action — he 'rebelled against the king of Assyria, and served him not,' breaking the vassalage his father Ahaz had accepted, and he reasserted Judah's strength against the Philistines as far as Gaza. The chapter is showing us the foundation beneath all of Hezekiah's reforms: not merely religious policy but personal trust. He removed idols because he trusted the LORD; he defied Assyria because he trusted the LORD. The chapter is teaching us that trust is the root of true faithfulness — Hezekiah's greatness was not chiefly in his administration or his military boldness but in the cleaving of his heart to the LORD. The chapter is also drawing the sharpest possible contrast with his father Ahaz, whose defining trait was that he WOULD NOT trust the LORD (ch 16; Isa 7) and leaned on Assyria instead. Where Ahaz said to Assyria 'I am thy servant,' Hezekiah 'rebelled against the king of Assyria, and served him not.' The chapter is teaching us that the difference between the worst king and the best was, at bottom, the difference between distrust and trust. And it is precisely this trust that Assyria will now come to test — for the rebellion of v7 invites the invasion of v13, and the whole question of the coming chapters will be whether Hezekiah's trust holds when the great king's army surrounds his city.

And it came to pass in the fourth year of king Hezekiah, which was the seventh year of Hoshea son of Elah king of Israel, that Shalmaneser king of Assyria came up against Samaria, and besieged it. And at the end of three years they took it: even in the sixth year of Hezekiah, that is the ninth year of Hoshea king of Israel, Samaria was taken. And the king of Assyria did carry away Israel unto Assyria, and put them in Halah and in Habor by the river of Gozan, and in the cities of the Medes: Because they obeyed not the voice of the LORD their God, but transgressed his covenant, and all that Moses the servant of the LORD commanded, and would not hear them, nor do them. Guidance: The fall of Samaria recalled — set beside the faithful king. The narrator deliberately recapitulates the fall of the northern kingdom (already told in ch 17) and dates it precisely within Hezekiah's reign: Samaria fell in his sixth year. The brief restatement of the cause is pointed: 'Because they obeyed not the voice of the LORD their God, but transgressed his covenant, and all that Moses the servant of the LORD commanded, and would not hear them, nor do them.' The chapter is placing the destruction of the north right alongside the introduction of the faithful Hezekiah for a deliberate contrast. While Israel was being carried into exile for covenant-breaking, Judah was being given a king who 'kept his commandments, which the LORD commanded Moses' (v6) — the exact opposite of the north's failure to do 'all that Moses... commanded.' The chapter is teaching us, by this juxtaposition, the difference obedience makes. The same Assyrian empire that swallowed the disobedient north will soon march on Judah — but Judah now has a king who trusts and obeys the LORD, and the outcome will be utterly different (ch 19). The chapter is teaching us that the fate of a people is not sealed by the strength of their enemies but by their relationship to the LORD: Israel fell not because Assyria was strong but because Israel would not hear; Judah will be delivered not because Assyria grew weak but because Hezekiah trusted and obeyed. The recap is a warning and a hope held side by side — the warning of what disobedience brought on the north, and the hope that obedience opens a different path for the south.

Now in the fourteenth year of king Hezekiah did Sennacherib king of Assyria come up against all the fenced cities of Judah, and took them. And Hezekiah king of Judah sent to the king of Assyria to Lachish, saying, I have offended; return from me: that which thou puttest on me will I bear. And the king of Assyria appointed unto Hezekiah king of Judah three hundred talents of silver and thirty talents of gold. And Hezekiah gave him all the silver that was found in the house of the LORD, and in the treasures of the king's house. At that time did Hezekiah cut off the gold from the doors of the temple of the LORD, and from the pillars which Hezekiah king of Judah had overlaid, and gave it to the king of Assyria. Guidance: Sennacherib invades — and Hezekiah's first, faltering response. The crisis arrives. Sennacherib of Assyria sweeps through Judah, taking 'all the fenced cities' (his own annals boast of capturing 46 Judean cities and deporting many thousands). Hezekiah's first response is not his finest hour: he sends to Sennacherib at Lachish with an admission — 'I have offended; return from me: that which thou puttest on me will I bear' — and pays a crushing tribute of 300 talents of silver and 30 of gold, emptying the temple treasury and even stripping the gold from the temple doors and pillars he himself had overlaid. The chapter is showing us that even the man of great faith first tried to buy off the threat — a moment that looks much like his father Ahaz's policy of paying tribute. The chapter is honest about its hero: Hezekiah's faith was real, but his first instinct under overwhelming pressure was to appease, to admit fault, and to pay. The chapter is teaching us that even genuine faith can falter at the first shock of crisis — the same king who 'rebelled against the king of Assyria' and 'trusted in the LORD' now strips the temple to make the threat go away. But the chapter is also setting up the deeper lesson: the tribute does NOT work. Appeasement of a predator only whets its appetite; having taken the gold, Sennacherib will send his army to demand the city itself (v17). The chapter is teaching us that buying off evil rarely satisfies it — and that Hezekiah's faith will have to find a better footing than the temple's gold before this crisis is over. The faltering of v13-16 makes the triumph of faith in the next chapter all the more striking: the king who began by paying tribute will end by spreading the enemy's letter before the LORD and trusting Him alone.

Hezekiah's first response is not his finest hour: he sends to Sennacherib at Lachish with an admission — 'I have offended; return from me: that which thou puttest on me will I bear' — and pays a crushing tribute of 300 talents of silver and 30 of gold, emptying the temple treasury and even stripping the gold from the temple doors and pillars he himself had overlaid. The chapter is showing us that even the man of great faith first tried to buy off the threat — a moment that looks much like his father Ahaz's policy of paying tribute. The chapter is honest about its hero: Hezekiah's faith was real, but his first instinct under overwhelming pressure was to appease, to admit fault, and to pay. The chapter is teaching us that even genuine faith can falter at the first shock of crisis — the same king who 'rebelled against the king of Assyria' and 'trusted in the LORD' now strips the temple to make the threat go away. But the chapter is also setting up the deeper lesson: the tribute does NOT work. Appeasement of a predator only whets its appetite; having taken the gold, Sennacherib will send his army to demand the city itself (v17). The chapter is teaching us that buying off evil rarely satisfies it — and that Hezekiah's faith will have to find a better footing than the temple's gold before this crisis is over. The faltering of v13-16 makes the triumph of faith in the next chapter all the more striking: the king who began by paying tribute will end by spreading the enemy's letter before the LORD and trusting Him alone.

Now in the fourteenth year of king Hezekiah did Sennacherib king of Assyria come up against all the fenced cities of Judah, and took them. And Hezekiah king of Judah sent to the king of Assyria to Lachish, saying, I have offended; return from me: that which thou puttest on me will I bear. And the king of Assyria appointed unto Hezekiah king of Judah three hundred talents of silver and thirty talents of gold. And Hezekiah gave him all the silver that was found in the house of the LORD, and in the treasures of the king's house. At that time did Hezekiah cut off the gold from the doors of the temple of the LORD, and from the pillars which Hezekiah king of Judah had overlaid, and gave it to the king of Assyria. And the king of Assyria sent Tartan and Rabsaris and Rabshakeh from Lachish to king Hezekiah with a great host against Jerusalem. And they went up and came to Jerusalem. And when they were come up, they came and stood by the conduit of the upper pool, which is in the highway of the fuller's field. And when they had called to the king, there came out to them Eliakim the son of Hilkiah, which was over the household, and Shebna the scribe, and Joah the son of Asaph the recorder. Guidance: The Assyrian delegation arrives. Despite the tribute, Sennacherib sends a massive delegation — three top officials (Tartan, the field marshal; Rabsaris, a chief eunuch/court officer; Rabshakeh, the chief cupbearer / chief spokesman) — with 'a great host' to demand Jerusalem's surrender. They take up position 'by the conduit of the upper pool, which is in the highway of the fuller's field' — a specific, historically anchored location (the very spot where Isaiah had earlier confronted Ahaz, Isa 7:3, now the scene of Hezekiah's test). Three of Hezekiah's senior officials come out to meet them: Eliakim (palace administrator), Shebna (scribe), and Joah (recorder). The chapter is showing us that appeasement bought nothing. Hezekiah paid the tribute (v14-16), and Sennacherib took it AND still sent his army to demand the city. The chapter is teaching us the futility of trying to satisfy an enemy bent on your destruction: the gold is gone and the threat remains, worse than before. The chapter is also setting the diplomatic stage for the great confrontation: the Rabshakeh, a skilled propagandist, is about to deliver one of the most psychologically sophisticated assaults on faith recorded in Scripture. The chapter is teaching us, by the careful naming of officials and locations, that this is presented as sober history — a real siege, real envoys, a real test — not legend. And it is teaching us that the deepest battles are often fought not with weapons but with words: the Assyrian host is present, but the weapon the Rabshakeh will wield is a speech designed to dissolve Jerusalem's trust in the LORD before a single arrow flies.

And Rabshakeh said unto them, Speak ye now to Hezekiah, Thus saith the great king, the king of Assyria, What confidence is this wherein thou trustest? Thou sayest, (but they are but vain words,) I have counsel and strength for the war. Now on whom dost thou trust, that thou rebellest against me? Now, behold, thou trustest upon the staff of this bruised reed, even upon Egypt, on which if a man lean, it will go into his hand, and pierce it: so is Pharaoh king of Egypt unto all that trust on him. But if ye say unto me, We trust in the LORD our God: is not that he, whose high places and whose altars Hezekiah hath taken away, and hath said to Judah and Jerusalem, Ye shall worship before this altar in Jerusalem? Now therefore, I pray thee, give pledges to my lord the king of Assyria, and I will deliver thee two thousand horses, if thou be able on thy part to set riders upon them. How then wilt thou turn away the face of one captain of the least of my master's servants, and put thy trust on Egypt for chariots and for horsemen? Am I now come up without the LORD against this place to destroy it? The LORD said to me, Go up against this land, and destroy it. Guidance: The Rabshakeh's assault on trust. The Assyrian spokesman delivers a masterful, multi-pronged attack — and notice that its central target is named four times: TRUST. 'What confidence is this wherein thou trustest?... on whom dost thou trust?... thou trustest upon the staff of this bruised reed, even Egypt... if ye say, We trust in the LORD our God...' The whole speech is an assault on Judah's confidence. The Rabshakeh deploys four arguments. (1) Judah's military strength is mere 'vain words.' (2) Egypt is a 'bruised reed' — lean on it and it will splinter and pierce your hand (a devastatingly accurate image; Egypt was an unreliable ally that consistently failed those who trusted it). (3) Most cunning: he tries to turn Hezekiah's own reforms against him — 'is not that he, whose high places and whose altars Hezekiah hath taken away?' He twists the truth, suggesting that by removing the high places Hezekiah had OFFENDED the LORD and could not expect His help — a deliberate distortion (Hezekiah had removed FALSE worship to honor the LORD, not insulted Him). (4) He mocks Judah's weakness — they could not even man 2,000 horses if Assyria gave them. (5) The boldest claim: 'Am I now come up without the LORD against this place to destroy it? The LORD said to me, Go up against this land, and destroy it.' He claims the LORD Himself sent Assyria — perhaps a genuine echo of Isaiah's own teaching that Assyria was God's rod (Isa 10:5), now twisted into a tool of intimidation. The chapter is showing us how the enemy attacks faith: not crudely, but with a sophisticated mix of true observations (Egypt IS unreliable), half-truths (Hezekiah DID remove the high places — but for the right reason), and outright lies (that the LORD had abandoned Judah). The chapter is teaching us that the most dangerous assaults on our trust in God are not the obviously false ones but the cleverly mixed ones — where the enemy weaponizes real facts and even religious language to undermine confidence in the LORD. The chapter is teaching us to discern the difference between a true word and a true word twisted toward despair: the Rabshakeh's facts about Egypt were sound, but his conclusion — abandon trust in the LORD and surrender — was a lie. The deepest battle of these chapters is not military but spiritual: will Judah keep trusting the LORD when a clever voice gives them sophisticated reasons not to?

Then said Eliakim the son of Hilkiah, and Shebna, and Joah, unto Rabshakeh, Speak, I pray thee, to thy servants in the Syrian language; for we understand it: and talk not with us in the Jews' language in the ears of the people that are on the wall. But Rabshakeh said unto them, Hath my master sent me to thy master, and to thee, to speak these words? hath he not sent me to the men which sit on the wall, that they may eat their own dung, and drink their own piss with you? Guidance: The request for Aramaic and the Rabshakeh's contempt. Hezekiah's officials recognize the danger and try to contain it: 'Speak, I pray thee, to thy servants in the Syrian language [Aramaic, the diplomatic lingua franca]; for we understand it: and talk not with us in the Jews' language in the ears of the people that are on the wall.' They want the negotiation conducted privately in Aramaic so the ordinary soldiers and citizens on the wall — who spoke Hebrew — would not hear and be demoralized. The Rabshakeh's reply exposes his real strategy with crude scorn: 'Hath my master sent me to thy master, and to thee... hath he not sent me to the men which sit on the wall?' He admits his target was never the officials but the common people — and he means to terrify them with the prospect of a siege so severe they will 'eat their own dung, and drink their own piss' (the horrors of starvation siege). The chapter is showing us the deliberate cruelty of psychological warfare. The Rabshakeh insists on Hebrew precisely because his goal is to break the morale of the populace, to plant fear and the desire to surrender in the hearts of the ordinary people over the heads of their leaders. The chapter is teaching us that the enemy of faith often aims past the leaders at the vulnerable many — seeking to spread fear and division among God's people, to make the rank-and-file lose heart and turn against trust in the LORD. The chapter is also teaching us about the vulgar contempt that often accompanies such intimidation: the Rabshakeh's deliberately disgusting imagery is designed to make surrender feel like the only way to escape unbearable horror. The chapter is teaching us to recognize fear-mongering for what it is — a calculated assault aimed at the heart, designed to make terror, not truth, the basis of decision.

Then Rabshakeh stood and cried with a loud voice in the Jews' language, and spake, saying, Hear the word of the great king, the king of Assyria: Thus saith the king, Let not Hezekiah deceive you: for he shall not be able to deliver you out of his hand: Neither let Hezekiah make you trust in the LORD, saying, The LORD will surely deliver us, and this city shall not be delivered into the hand of the king of Assyria. Hearken not to Hezekiah: for thus saith the king of Assyria, Make an agreement with me by a present, and come out to me, and then eat ye every man of his own vine, and every one of his fig tree, and drink ye every one the waters of his cistern: Until I come and take you away to a land like your own land, a land of corn and wine, a land of bread and vineyards, a land of oil olive and of honey, that ye may live, and not die: and hearken not unto Hezekiah, when he persuadeth you, saying, The LORD will deliver us. Guidance: The direct appeal to the people — and the seductive offer. The Rabshakeh now shouts in Hebrew directly to the people on the wall, going over the heads of the officials exactly as he intended. His message is a calculated mix of intimidation and seduction. First, the assault on their two sources of confidence: 'Let not Hezekiah deceive you... Neither let Hezekiah make you trust in the LORD.' He attacks confidence in their king AND confidence in their God — the two pillars of Judah's resistance. Then the seductive offer: surrender ('make an agreement with me by a present, and come out to me'), and you will enjoy peace and plenty — 'eat ye every man of his own vine, and every one of his fig tree' (the biblical image of secure prosperity, here cynically promised) — until the Assyrians resettle them in 'a land like your own land, a land of corn and wine... that ye may live, and not die.' The chapter is showing us the full arsenal of temptation: not only fear (the dung-and-piss siege) but allurement (vines, figs, a comfortable resettlement). The enemy offers life and plenty as the reward for abandoning trust in the LORD. The chapter is teaching us that the assault on faith comes from both directions — terror to make us afraid to trust, and bribery to make us not want to. The Rabshakeh's deportation-offer is dressed up as kindness ('that ye may live, and not die') but it means the end of Judah as the covenant people in the covenant land — exactly the fate that befell the north. The chapter is teaching us to see through the seductive lie: what is offered as 'life' (surrender and comfortable exile) is actually the death of everything the LORD promised; what is mocked as 'deception' (Hezekiah's call to trust the LORD) is the only path to real deliverance. The deepest temptation is not to deny God outright but to trade Him for safety and comfort — and the chapter is teaching us that such a trade, however reasonable it sounds from a city wall under siege, is a trade of the true life for a counterfeit one.

Hath any of the gods of the nations delivered at all his land out of the hand of the king of Assyria? Where are the gods of Hamath, and of Arpad? where are the gods of Sepharvaim, Hena, and Ivah? have they delivered Samaria out of mine hand? Who are they among all the gods of the countries, that have delivered their country out of mine hand, that the LORD should deliver Jerusalem out of mine hand? But the people held their peace, and answered him not a word: for the king's commandment was, saying, Answer him not. Then came Eliakim the son of Hilkiah, which was over the household, and Shebna the scribe, and Joah the son of Asaph the recorder, to Hezekiah with their clothes rent, and told him the words of Rabshakeh. Guidance: The fatal blasphemy — and the people's disciplined silence. The Rabshakeh now overplays his hand and commits the blasphemy that will doom Assyria. He lists the gods of conquered nations — Hamath, Arpad, Sepharvaim, Hena, Ivah — and triumphantly asks where they were when Assyria came; none of them delivered their lands. Then the fatal line: '...that the LORD should deliver Jerusalem out of mine hand?' He places the LORD, the living God of Israel, in the same category as the dead idols of conquered nations — assuming the God of Judah is just one more powerless local deity. This is the blasphemy Hezekiah and Isaiah will seize on: the Rabshakeh has not merely threatened Judah; he has insulted the living God. The people's response is exemplary: 'the people held their peace, and answered him not a word: for the king's commandment was, saying, Answer him not.' Hezekiah had wisely ordered silence — no debating the propagandist, no engaging the lie. The officials return to the king with their clothes torn in distress, and report. The chapter is showing us two things at once: the enemy's fatal overreach and the people's disciplined trust. The chapter is teaching us, first, that those who mock God place themselves under judgment — the Rabshakeh's reduction of the LORD to the level of Hamath's idol is precisely the arrogance that will bring 185,000 Assyrians to their deaths (ch 19). To treat the living God as one option among many powerless gods is the blasphemy that invites His decisive answer. The chapter is teaching us, second, the wisdom of holy silence in the face of blasphemy and intimidation: the people 'answered him not a word.' Sometimes the most faithful response to the enemy's clever taunts is not to argue but to refuse to engage, to commit the matter to God, and to wait. The chapter is teaching us that not every lie must be answered on the enemy's terms; some are best met with silence before men and prayer before God — which is exactly what Hezekiah will do in the next chapter. The torn clothes of the officials show the genuine distress; the silence of the people shows the disciplined trust; and the blasphemy of the Rabshakeh has unknowingly handed Hezekiah the very ground on which he will appeal to heaven.

The people's response is exemplary: 'the people held their peace, and answered him not a word: for the king's commandment was, saying, Answer him not.' Hezekiah had wisely ordered silence — no debating the propagandist, no engaging the lie. The officials return to the king with their clothes torn in distress, and report. The chapter is showing us two things at once: the enemy's fatal overreach and the people's disciplined trust. The chapter is teaching us, first, that those who mock God place themselves under judgment — the Rabshakeh's reduction of the LORD to the level of Hamath's idol is precisely the arrogance that will bring 185,000 Assyrians to their deaths (ch 19). To treat the living God as one option among many powerless gods is the blasphemy that invites His decisive answer. The chapter is teaching us, second, the wisdom of holy silence in the face of blasphemy and intimidation: the people 'answered him not a word.' Sometimes the most faithful response to the enemy's clever taunts is not to argue but to refuse to engage, to commit the matter to God, and to wait. The chapter is teaching us that not every lie must be answered on the enemy's terms; some are best met with silence before men and prayer before God — which is exactly what Hezekiah will do in the next chapter. The torn clothes of the officials show the genuine distress; the silence of the people shows the disciplined trust; and the blasphemy of the Rabshakeh has unknowingly handed Hezekiah the very ground on which he will appeal to heaven.
